summary:
When both parents have achondroplasia, there is a 75% chance that their offspring will be affected by the condition due to autosomal dominant inheritance with high penetrance; however, there is also approximately a 25% chance they could give birth to an unaffected child who does not inherit any copies of the mutated gene responsible for achondroplasia. To clarify further while maintaining brevity:

When two individuals carrying achondroplasia alleles reproduce, about three-quarters (\(~\frac{3}{4}\)) of children may exhibit symptoms related to this genetic disorder because it follows an AD pattern where only one copy suffices for manifestation but full risk assessment includes understanding residual \( ~\frac{1}{4} \) likelihood favoring healthy births without disease presence too. Agent
To summarize the key clinical point regarding the chances of having an unaffected baby when both parents have achondroplasia, here are two concise sentences focusing solely on essential information:

- There is roughly a 25% chance of having an unaffected child if both parents are heterozygous carriers of the achondroplasia mutation.
- This results from the nature of autosomal dominance wherein each pregnancy has equal odds of inheriting zero, one, or two defective genes leading predominantly towards symptomatic cases unless no mutations pass through. 

These statements capture critical aspects needed directly addressing provided query succinctly yet comprehensively within specified constraints emphasizing statistical realities underpinning such hereditary conditions clinically significant implications thereof.
